reasoning: The finding correctly identifies that `encryptionPublicCert` is interpolated without escaping at keyinfo.tpl.xml.js line 10, and that `pemToCert` uses a regex `[^-]*` that does NOT filter `<`/`>` characters. Technically, a PEM string containing XML metacharacters in the cert body could inject XML into the KeyInfo block. However, the reachability is not real in any externally-exploitable sense: `options.pem` is passed by the application operator as their own certificate at encryption time — it is operator-controlled configuration, not user-controlled or attacker-controlled input. There is no API surface through which a remote or untrusted party can influence `options.pem`. The library documents this as a required, trusted certificate parameter. The 'attacker' in this scenario is the operator themselves, making this a self-injection with no security boundary crossing. Additionally, a well-formed PEM cert contains only base64 characters (A-Za-z0-9+/=) plus newlines, so in normal use the unescaped interpolation is safe. Only a deliberately malformed or adversarially crafted PEM string supplied by the operator would trigger injection. No schema validator, no XML escaping, and no sanitizer is present on this parameter, but none is needed given the trust level of the input. The finding is technically observable in the code but does not represent a real exploitable vulnerability given the intended threat model.
